answer:
**Charles Comte's *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, Chapter 13 ("Influence du déboisement des montagnes sur les fleuves et les rivières"), cites Arthur Young's *Travels in France* (1787–1789) as eyewitness testimony to forest destruction in the Pyrenees.**[^1]

Comte introduces Young as a traveler who "was witness to devastations that he would not have believed possible" and "bitterly deplored," specifically in the Pyrenees, noting Young's pro-agricultural bias made his account even more credible.[^1] Young is then quoted describing woods near Bagnères de Luchon where "men were at work, cutting and splitting young birches, to make barrel hoops," before lamenting the forest of Lartigues:

> This beautiful and noble forest of Lartigues has experienced a devastation so general, that it is almost entirely destroyed; there are no young shoots to replace the trees that have disappeared, and, in ten or twelve years, it will be nothing more than a bare mountain, with some miserable shrubs browsed by goats or by other animals.[^2]

The quote continues with further observations of devastation nearby.[^3]

[^1]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 13](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-4ce3c5).

[^2]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 13](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-5e30bb).

[^3]: Charles Comte, *Traité de la propriété: VOL I*, [Chapter 13](/works/charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99/13-influence-du-deboisement-des-montagnes-sur-les-fle/translation/#p-2d6f5a).
